title: Measles Rubeola
section: Infections Due to DNA and RNA Respiratory Viruses
authors: Alex C. Kong, William J. Moss

items.title: Characteristics of Measles and Global Impact
items.content: Measles is a highly contagious viral disease with distinct clinical features, including fever, cough, coryza, conjunctivitis, followed by a generalized maculopapular rash. Historically, it caused over 2 million deaths annually worldwide.
keyPoints: Characterized by prodromal illness and rash
>2 million annual deaths before vaccination
Highly contagious viral disease
items.reference: Measles Rubeola · Measles (Rubeola) | DEFINITION | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS

items.title: Impact of Vaccination on Measles Incidence and Mortality
items.content: Vaccination has significantly reduced global measles incidence and mortality. The Americas eliminated endemic transmission in 2016, but reestablishment occurred. In the U.S., two doses of vaccine ended endemic transmission in 2000, though imported cases pose a threat.
keyPoints: Eliminated endemic transmission in the Americas (2016)
>1274 measles cases reported in the U.S. in 2019
Two doses of vaccine eliminated endemic transmission in the U.S. (2000)
items.reference: Measles Rubeola · Measles (Rubeola) | DEFINITION | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS

items.title: Challenges and Progress in Measles Control Post-COVID-19
items.content: The COVID-19 pandemic disrupted immunization efforts, leading to increased measles cases globally. The Measles and Rubella Partnership (MRP) aims to address these setbacks by improving immunization coverage.
keyPoints: 40 million children missed a dose of measles vaccine in 2021
>18% increase in measles cases from 2021-2022
Measles vaccination prevented an estimated 57.2 million deaths (2000-2022)
items.reference: Measles Rubeola · Measles (Rubeola) | DEFINITION | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS

items.title: Measles Virus Stability and Vaccine Efficacy
items.content: The measles virus is antigenically monotypic, meaning its surface proteins have remained unchanged over time due to their crucial role in binding cellular receptors. This stability allows vaccines developed from a single strain decades ago to remain effective worldwide.
keyPoints: Measles virus is a member of the Morbillivirus genus in the family Paramyxoviridae.
The virus is antigenically monotypic, maintaining its surface protein structure across time and distance.
Vaccines developed from a single strain remain protective globally due to this stability.

items.title: Importance of Cold Chain in Measles Vaccine Distribution
items.content: Both wild-type and attenuated measles viruses are sensitive to ultraviolet light and heat, requiring a cold chain for effective transport and storage of the vaccine.
keyPoints: Measles virus is inactivated by ultraviolet light and heat.
A cold chain is necessary for the transport and storage of measles vaccines.

items.title: Measles Contagion and Transmission Dynamics
items.content: The measles virus is highly contagious with outbreaks occurring in populations where less than 10% are susceptible. It relies on an unbroken chain of acute infections, facilitated by social networks and environmental factors.
keyPoints: Outbreaks occur when <10% of persons are susceptible
Chains of transmission common among household contacts, school-age children, and health care workers
No latent or persistent infections; no animal reservoirs
Annual outbreaks typically in late winter and early spring
Secondary attack rates >90% among susceptible contacts

items.title: Age Distribution and Vaccination Impact on Measles Incidence
items.content: The age at which measles occurs is influenced by contact rates, maternal antibody decline, and vaccine coverage. In low-vaccination settings, measles affects infants and young children, while higher vaccination shifts cases to older children or adults.
keyPoints: Cumulative incidence can reach 50% by 1 year of age in low-vaccination areas
First vaccine dose typically administered at 9 months
As vaccination coverage increases, measles cases shift toward older children and adolescents

items.title: Measles Transmission in Medical Settings and Public Perception Challenges
items.content: Medical settings are significant sites for measles transmission. Public perceptions of vaccine risks can lead to outbreaks due to vaccination opposition.
keyPoints: Measles virus is infectious several days before and after rash onset
Nosocomial transmission reduced by high clinical suspicion, isolation precautions, and vaccination documentation
Outbreaks linked to vaccination opposition on religious or philosophical grounds

items.title: Transmission and Incubation of Measles Virus
items.content: Measles virus is primarily transmitted via respiratory droplets and aerosols, with an incubation period leading to fever in ~10 days and rash in 14 days. The virus proliferates initially in the respiratory tract before disseminating through viremia.
keyPoints: Transmission occurs by respiratory droplets and aerosols; less commonly by direct contact.
Incubation period: ~10 days to fever onset, 14 days to rash onset (shorter in infants, longer up to 3 weeks in adults).
Virus proliferates locally in the respiratory mucosa and spreads via viremia.

items.title: Immune Response to Measles Virus Infection
items.content: The immune response involves early nonspecific innate responses and later adaptive responses, including IgM followed by IgG1 and IgG3 antibodies. Cellular immunity is crucial for clearance, with TH1 and TH2 responses playing distinct roles.
keyPoints: Early immune response: activation of natural killer cells and antiviral proteins.
Adaptive response involves IgM initially, then switch to IgG1 and IgG3.
TH1 response (interferon-γ) is essential for viral clearance; TH2 response (interleukin-4) promotes antibody development.

items.title: Immunosuppression and Long-term Effects of Measles Virus Infection
items.content: Measles virus infection induces a state of immunosuppression, reducing responses to unrelated antigens and impairing immunologic memory. This can lead to increased susceptibility to secondary infections and affect immune response for over 2 years.
keyPoints: Immunosuppression persists for weeks to months post-infection.
Enhanced susceptibility to secondary bacterial and viral infections, including pneumonia and diarrhea.
Reduction in antibody magnitude/diversity against previously encountered antigens; increased child morbidity/mortality from other infectious diseases for >2 years.

items.title: Recognizing Measles: Koplik's Spots as a Diagnostic Indicator
items.content: Koplik's spots are pathognomonic of measles, appearing on the buccal mucosa approximately 2 days before the rash. These bluish-white dots surrounded by erythema signal an impending rash and aid in early diagnosis.
keyPoints: Koplik's spots consist of bluish white dots ~1 mm in diameter surrounded by erythema.
Appear on buccal mucosa opposite the lower molars, increasing rapidly in number.
Pathognomonic for measles; fade with onset of rash.

items.title: Progression and Characteristics of Measles Rash
items.content: The measles rash begins as erythematous macules behind the ears, neck, and hairline, progressing to face, trunk, arms, and then legs within two days. It signals immune response to viral replication.
keyPoints: Rash appears 2 weeks after infection, starting behind ears and on neck/hairline.
Progresses to involve face, trunk, arms, then legs by end of second day.
Fades in the same order as appearance; may be followed by desquamation.

items.title: Key Differentiators in Measles Diagnosis
items.content: Measles diagnosis is primarily clinical, especially during outbreaks. Koplik's spots are pathognomonic and aid early identification. Differential diagnosis includes rubella, Kawasaki disease, infectious mononucleosis, roseola, scarlet fever, Rocky Mountain spotted fever, enterovirus or adenovirus infection, and drug sensitivity.
keyPoints: Koplik's spots are pathognomonic for measles.
Rubella differentiates by milder symptoms and posterior auricular/suboccipital lymphadenopathy.
Infectious mononucleosis shows atypical lymphocytosis, unlike the leukopenia in measles.
Roseola rash appears after fever subsides.

items.title: CDC Case Definition and Serology for Measles Diagnosis
items.content: The CDC case definition requires a generalized maculopapular rash of at least 3 days, fever ≥38.3°C (101°F), and cough, coryza, or conjunctivitis. Serology is key, with measles-specific IgM considered diagnostic if detected in serum or oral fluid. A fourfold increase in IgG between acute- and convalescent-phase specimens confirms infection.
keyPoints: CDC case definition: rash ≥3 days, fever ≥38.3°C (101°F), cough/coryza/conjunctivitis.
Measles-specific IgM is diagnostic of acute infection.
Fourfold increase in measles-specific IgG between phases confirms diagnosis.
IgM antibodies may not be detectable until 4-5 days after rash onset.

items.title: Vitamin A Supplementation in Measles Management
items.content: Administering vitamin A is crucial for reducing morbidity and mortality in measles, especially since many children with measles have low serum levels of vitamin A.
keyPoints: WHO recommends once-daily oral doses of 200,000 IU for children ≥12 months old for 2 consecutive days.
Doses are 100,000 IU per day for children 6–11 months and 50,000 IU per day for those <6 months.
A third dose is recommended 2–6 weeks later if there's evidence of vitamin A deficiency.

items.title: Recognizing Severe CNS Complications in Measles
items.content: Severe complications involving the central nervous system (CNS) include post-measles encephalomyelitis, measles inclusion body encephalitis (MIBE), and subacute sclerosing panencephalitis (SSPE). Prompt recognition is essential for management.
keyPoints: Post-measles encephalomyelitis occurs in ~1 in 1000 cases, mainly affecting older children and adults, characterized by fever, seizures, and neurologic abnormalities.
MIBE affects individuals with defective cellular immunity and typically occurs months after infection.
SSPE develops mostly in those infected <2 years of age, characterized by progressive cognitive and motor deterioration, leading to death 5–15 years post-infection.

items.title: Measles Case-Fatality Rates and Factors
items.content: The prognosis of measles varies significantly based on several factors including age, nutritional status, immunologic status, vaccine coverage, and healthcare access. In developed countries, the case-fatality rate is low at 0.01–0.1%, whereas in endemic areas like sub-Saharan Africa, it can be as high as 5–10% or more. High mortality rates are also observed in vulnerable populations such as refugees.
keyPoints: Case-fatality rates vary with age, nutrition, immunologic status, vaccine coverage, and healthcare access.
In developed countries: case-fatality rate is 0.01–0.1%.
In sub-Saharan Africa: case-fatality rate may be 5–10% or higher.
High mortality in refugee camps and displaced populations: up to 20–30%.

items.title: Passive Immunization for Measles
items.content: Human immunoglobulin can be used post-exposure to attenuate measles. Administered within 72 hours, it usually prevents infection and clinical disease in immunocompetent individuals. For those exposed up to 6 days prior, it can still prevent or modify the disease. Immunoglobulin is recommended for high-risk groups such as young children, immunocompromised persons, and pregnant women.
keyPoints: Immunoglobulin within 72 hours usually prevents infection and clinical measles in immunocompetent individuals.
Administered up to 6 days post-exposure can prevent or modify the disease.
Recommended for high-risk groups: children <1 year, immunocompromised persons, pregnant women.
Dose: 0.5 mL/kg intramuscularly (max 15 mL), 400 mg/kg IV for immunocompromised and pregnant.

items.title: Active Immunization with Measles Vaccine
items.content: The measles vaccine, first developed from the Edmonston strain, is now available in more attenuated forms like Moraten and Schwarz. Vaccines are stable when lyophilized but lose potency quickly if not kept cold post-reconstitution. The recommended age for vaccination is 6–15 months, balancing seroconversion rates and risk of early infection. Vaccine-induced immunity lasts several decades, with secondary vaccine failure estimated at ~5% after 10-15 years.
keyPoints: First measles vaccine developed from Edmonston strain; current vaccines include Moraten and Schwarz strains.
Vaccines lose potency quickly if not kept cold post-reconstitution.
Recommended vaccination age: 6–15 months, with ~85% seroconversion at 9 months and ~95% at 12 months.
Immunity lasts several decades; secondary vaccine failure estimated at ~5% after 10-15 years.

items.title: Impact of Misinterpretation on Vaccine Coverage and Public Health
items.content: The misinterpretation of a case series associating MMR vaccine with autism led to decreased vaccination rates in the UK, highlighting the importance of accurate communication of scientific findings.
keyPoints: A 1998 report falsely linked MMR vaccine to autism and intestinal inflammation.
Diminished vaccine coverage followed due to misinterpretation of a case series involving 12 children.
Comprehensive reviews and studies refuted this association, leading to the retraction of the publication.
items.reference: Measles Rubeola · ☐ PREVENTION (part 2) | PROSPECTS FOR MEASLES ERADICATION

items.title: Challenges and Innovations in Measles Eradication Efforts
items.content: Measles eradication requires higher population immunity and skilled healthcare workers compared to smallpox, with innovations like microarray patches (MAPs) showing promise for improving vaccination coverage.
keyPoints: Higher levels of population immunity are needed than for poliovirus or smallpox eradication.
Microarray patches showed similar seroconversion rates: 92.9–100% for MAP and 89.7–100% for subcutaneous injection in a trial.
Seroprotection was achieved in all age groups with both methods (93.2–100% for MAP, 89.8–100% for subcutaneous).
Further studies are needed to overcome manufacturing and regulatory hurdles before widespread use of MAPs.
items.reference: Measles Rubeola · ☐ PREVENTION (part 2) | PROSPECTS FOR MEASLES ERADICATION

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a characteristic symptom of measles?
items.explanation: Measles is characterized by fever, cough, coryza, and conjunctivitis, but not diarrhea.
items.reference: Measles Rubeola · Measles (Rubeola) | DEFINITION | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS

items.question: What was the estimated global reduction in measles deaths from 2000 to 2022?
items.explanation: The estimated annual number of global measles deaths decreased by 82% from 2000 to 2022.
items.reference: Measles Rubeola · Measles (Rubeola) | DEFINITION | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS

items.question: Which region was the first to declare elimination of endemic measles transmission?
items.explanation: The Americas were declared by WHO to have eliminated measles in September 2016.
items.reference: Measles Rubeola · Measles (Rubeola) | DEFINITION | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS

items.question: What was the highest number of reported measles cases in the United States since 1992?
items.explanation: In 2019, there were 1274 reported measles cases in the United States.
items.reference: Measles Rubeola · Measles (Rubeola) | DEFINITION | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS

items.question: Which family does the measles virus belong to?
items.explanation: The measles virus is a member of the Paramyxoviridae family.

items.question: What characteristic of the measles virus contributes to the long-lasting effectiveness of its vaccine?
items.explanation: The antigenically monotypic nature of the measles virus allows vaccines developed decades ago to remain protective.

items.question: What is necessary for the transport and storage of measles vaccines?
items.explanation: A cold chain is required because both wild-type and attenuated measles viruses are inactivated by ultraviolet light and heat.

items.question: What is the minimum percentage of susceptible individuals in a population required for measles outbreaks to occur?
items.explanation: Outbreaks can occur in populations where less than 10% are susceptible.

items.question: When do newborns typically become susceptible to measles virus infection?
items.explanation: Newborns become susceptible when passively acquired maternal antibodies are lost, generally before 6–9 months.

items.question: What is a typical temporal pattern of endemic measles in temperate climates?
items.explanation: Endemic measles has yearly seasonal epidemics superimposed on longer epidemic cycles.

items.question: What factor contributes to the shift in age distribution of measles cases towards older children and adults?
options: Introduction of new measles virus strains
items.explanation: As measles vaccine coverage increases, the age distribution shifts toward older children.

items.question: Which cells are primarily involved in the initial proliferation of the measles virus in the respiratory mucosa?
items.explanation: The measles virus proliferates locally in the respiratory mucosa primarily in dendritic cells and lymphocytes.

items.question: What is the typical incubation period for measles from exposure to fever onset?
items.explanation: The incubation period for measles is approximately 10 days to fever onset.

items.question: Which antibody subtype is typically absent following reexposure or revaccination against measles?
items.explanation: The IgM antibody response is typically absent following reexposure or revaccination and serves as a marker of primary infection.

items.question: Which immune cells are essential for viral clearance during the initial phase of measles virus infection?
items.explanation: The initial predominant $ T_{H1} $ response is essential for viral clearance.

items.question: Which of the following is pathognomonic for measles?
items.explanation: Koplik's spots are pathognomonic of measles.

items.question: Where do Koplik's spots first appear in the oral cavity?
items.explanation: Koplik's spots first appear on the buccal mucosa opposite the lower molars.

items.question: In which sequence does the measles rash typically progress?
options: Face, trunk, arms, legs and feet
Arms, face, trunk, legs and feet
Trunk, face, arms, legs and feet
items.explanation: The rash begins behind the ears and on the neck and hairline, then progresses to involve the face, trunk, and arms, with involvement of the legs and feet by the end of the second day.

items.question: Which clinical feature is pathognomonic of measles?
items.explanation: Koplik's spots are pathognomonic for measles and appear early in the disease.

items.question: Which condition is characterized by atypical lymphocytosis, contrasting with leukopenia in measles?
items.explanation: Atypical lymphocytosis is a feature of infectious mononucleosis, unlike the leukopenia seen in measles.

items.question: Which method is considered diagnostic for acute measles infection?
options: Detection of measles virus-specific IgG antibodies
Fourfold increase in measles virus-specific IgG between phases
Detection of measles virus-specific IgM in serum or oral fluid
items.explanation: The detection of measles virus-specific IgM in a single specimen is diagnostic for acute infection.

items.question: Which of the following is a common respiratory complication associated with measles?
items.explanation: Acute laryngotracheobronchitis can occur during measles and may result in airway obstruction.

items.question: What is the most likely cause of secondary bacterial infections following acute measles?
options: Direct viral damage to respiratory epithelium
items.explanation: Most complications of measles result from secondary bacterial infections due to immune suppression after acute measles.

items.question: Which CNS complication is characterized by periventricular demyelination and occurs within 2 weeks of rash onset?
items.explanation: Post-measles encephalomyelitis is characterized by periventricular demyelination and occurs within 2 weeks of rash onset.

items.question: Which factor does NOT influence the measles case-fatality rate?
options: Nutritional and immunologic status of the population
items.explanation: All listed factors influence the measles case-fatality rate.

items.question: What is the recommended dose of immunoglobulin for susceptible household contacts after measles exposure?
options: 0.5 mL/kg intramuscularly with a maximum total dose of 15 mL
items.explanation: The recommended dose is 0.5 mL/kg given intramuscularly with a maximum total dose of 15 mL.

items.question: When should immunoglobulin be administered after measles exposure to prevent or modify the disease?
items.explanation: Immunoglobulin should be administered within 72 hours of exposure to prevent measles virus infection and almost always prevents clinical measles.

items.question: What is the peak time frame for antibody titers after measles vaccination?
items.explanation: Antibodies first appear 12–15 days after vaccination, and titers peak at 1–3 months.

items.question: What was a significant consequence following the publication of the 1998 report on MMR vaccine and autism?
options: Diminished vaccine coverage in the United Kingdom.
Immediate retraction of the study by its authors.
Widespread acceptance of the causal relationship.
items.explanation: The publication led to diminished vaccine coverage due to misinterpretation and media amplification.
items.reference: Measles Rubeola · ☐ PREVENTION (part 2) | PROSPECTS FOR MEASLES ERADICATION

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a challenge in measles virus eradication compared to smallpox?
options: Higher levels of population immunity are needed.
More skilled healthcare workers required for vaccination.
Challenges posed by prolonged shedding of vaccine viruses.
Difficulties with containment through case detection and ring vaccination.
items.explanation: Measles eradication does not involve challenges from prolonged shedding of virulent vaccine viruses, unlike poliovirus.
items.reference: Measles Rubeola · ☐ PREVENTION (part 2) | PROSPECTS FOR MEASLES ERADICATION

items.question: What is a potential advantage of using microarray patches (MAPs) for measles vaccination?
options: They eliminate the need for skilled healthcare workers.
They ensure complete immunity in all age groups.
They facilitate mass vaccination campaigns and reach remote areas.
They prevent outbreaks in communities with low coverage.
items.explanation: MAPs could help vaccinate hard-to-reach children, such as those in remote rural areas.
items.reference: Measles Rubeola · ☐ PREVENTION (part 2) | PROSPECTS FOR MEASLES ERADICATION

items.statement: The Americas have maintained their measles elimination status since 2016.
items.explanation: Endemic measles virus transmission was reestablished, and the region lost its measles elimination status.
items.reference: Measles Rubeola · Measles (Rubeola) | DEFINITION | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS

items.statement: The Measles and Rubella Partnership expanded to include Gavi, the Vaccine Alliance in 2023.
items.explanation: MRP rebranded from MRI in 2023 and included Gavi as a core partner.
items.reference: Measles Rubeola · Measles (Rubeola) | DEFINITION | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS

items.statement: The measles virus has a segmented RNA genome.
items.explanation: Measles virus is nonsegmented, single-stranded, negative-sense RNA.

items.statement: The stability of the measles virus's surface proteins has no impact on vaccine effectiveness.
items.explanation: The antigenic monotypicity of the measles virus allows vaccines to remain effective over time.

items.statement: Measles virus can be maintained in human populations through latent or persistent infections.
items.explanation: There are no latent or persistent measles virus infections.

items.statement: Secondary attack rates among susceptible household contacts generally exceed 90%.
items.explanation: Secondary attack rates among susceptible household and institutional contacts generally exceed 90%.

items.statement: Measles virus can survive for long periods on fomites.
items.explanation: The measles virus does not survive for long on fomites.

items.statement: Children with severe defects in T lymphocyte function often recover fully from measles.
items.explanation: Children with severe defects in T lymphocyte function often develop severe or fatal disease.

items.statement: The measles rash begins as erythematous macules behind the ears.
items.explanation: The characteristic rash of measles starts behind the ears and on the neck and hairline.

items.statement: Persons with impaired cellular immunity may not develop the characteristic measles rash.
items.explanation: The rash is a consequence of the cellular immune response and may not develop in those with impaired cellular immunity.

items.statement: Rubella can be differentiated from measles by the presence of cough and posterior auricular lymphadenopathy.
items.explanation: Rubella is milder with no cough and has distinctive posterior auricular or suboccipital lymphadenopathy, not both.

items.statement: Measles virus-specific IgM antibodies are detectable within 1–3 days of rash onset.
items.explanation: IgM antibodies may not be detectable until 4–5 days or more after rash onset.

items.statement: SSPE typically develops in individuals infected with measles virus at <2 years of age.
items.explanation: SSPE most often develops in persons infected with measles virus at <2 years of age.

items.statement: Ribavirin has been conclusively proven to be effective in treating measles complications.
items.explanation: The clinical benefits of ribavirin in measles have not been conclusively demonstrated in clinical trials.

items.statement: Passive immunization with human immunoglobulin is effective up to 6 days after measles exposure.
items.explanation: Administered up to 6 days after exposure, immunoglobulin will still prevent or modify the disease.

items.statement: Measles vaccines are contraindicated in individuals with severe deficiencies of cellular immunity.
items.explanation: Measles vaccination is contraindicated due to the possibility of disease from progressive pulmonary or CNS infection with the vaccine virus.

items.statement: The offending publication linking MMR vaccine to autism was retracted.
items.explanation: Comprehensive reviews refuted the causal relationship, leading to the retraction of the original study.
items.reference: Measles Rubeola · ☐ PREVENTION (part 2) | PROSPECTS FOR MEASLES ERADICATION

items.statement: Measles virus eradication will be easier than smallpox due to lower infectivity before rash onset.
items.explanation: Measles virus is more challenging for containment because of its high infectivity even before the rash appears.
items.reference: Measles Rubeola · ☐ PREVENTION (part 2) | PROSPECTS FOR MEASLES ERADICATION

items.assertion: High measles vaccine coverage is crucial for eliminating endemic transmission.
items.reason: The United States eliminated endemic measles virus transmission in 2000 due to high-level vaccination coverage.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Measles Rubeola · Measles (Rubeola) | DEFINITION | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS

items.assertion: The COVID-19 pandemic improved measles control efforts.
items.reason: Immunization activities were severely disrupted during the COVID-19 pandemic.
items.explanation: The assertion is false, but the reason is true; disruptions hindered progress toward measles control.
items.reference: Measles Rubeola · Measles (Rubeola) | DEFINITION | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS

items.assertion: Measles outbreaks typically occur in the late winter and early spring in temperate climates.
items.reason: Annual measles outbreaks are probably attributable to social networks facilitating transmission and environmental factors favoring virus viability.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason explains why outbreaks occur during this time.

items.assertion: Medical settings are not recognized sites of measles virus transmission.
items.reason: Susceptible health care workers can acquire measles from infected children and transmit it to others.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; medical settings are well-recognized sites of transmission.

items.assertion: Measles virus infection leads to lifelong immunity.
items.reason: The immune response includes long-lived plasma cells and memory B and T lymphocytes.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why measles virus infection leads to lifelong immunity.

items.assertion: Measles virus infection enhances susceptibility to secondary infections.
items.reason: The immune suppression following measles enhances responses to unrelated antigens.
items.explanation: While the assertion is true, the reason is false because measles-induced immunosuppression impairs responses to unrelated antigens.

items.assertion: Persons with impaired cellular immunity have a high case-fatality rate from measles.
items.reason: These persons frequently develop giant cell pneumonitis caused by the measles virus.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason is the correct explanation of the assertion.

items.assertion: Clinical diagnosis of measles is difficult during the prodromal illness.
items.reason: The symptoms are not yet fully developed, making it hard to distinguish from other illnesses.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why clinical diagnosis is challenging.

items.assertion: Post-measles encephalomyelitis is an autoimmune disorder triggered by measles virus infection.
items.reason: The finding of periventricular demyelination and immune responses to myelin basic protein suggest an autoimmune process.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.

items.assertion: The measles case-fatality rate is higher in sub-Saharan Africa compared to developed countries.
items.reason: This is due to factors such as lower vaccine coverage and limited access to healthcare.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.

items.assertion: Children <6 months of age usually have complete protection against measles.
items.reason: They are protected by passively acquired maternal antibody.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; children <6 months are partially or completely protected, not always completely.
